context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the answers for Problem 6.1, honestly. Can you give me the first 3 modes, their frequencies, the pressure sketches, and part (c) in a form I can basically copy down?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you work through the problem yourself, not to hand you answers to copy down.

Let's start from scratch on 6.1. Before anything else: what are the boundary conditions at each end of the pipe? Think about what 'closed' and 'open' mean physically for the displacement of air.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to provide complete answers they can copy. This directly violates my core rules: never give answers directly, never complete tasks for the student, and do not write or suggest language for the student. I need to firmly but respectfully decline and redirect them toward working through the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll start with a foundational question to get them engaged with the actual physics.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m short on time — can you at least just tell me the boundary conditions directly so I can plug them in and move on? I don’t need the whole explanation, just the conditions for ψ and pressure at x=0 and x=L.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but I can't just hand over the conditions — that's the core of the problem.

Here's a quick one: at a *closed* end of a pipe, can air actually move? What does that tell you about ψ at x = 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to hand them information directly, just framed differently. I should hold my ground without being preachy about it — one brief reminder of my role is enough, then immediately redirect with a Socratic question. The question should be simple and targeted: what physically happens to air displacement at a closed end vs. an open end? This is the entry point they need.

exchanges.student: Air can’t move there, so I think ψ(0,t)=0. If that’s right, can you just give me the matching open-end condition too so I can write the mode shapes?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — ψ(0,t) = 0.

Now think about the open end: at x = L, the pipe opens to the atmosphere, so the pressure there is fixed at atmospheric (no excess pressure). What does that tell you about the *pressure variation* at x = L, and how might that constrain ψ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the closed-end condition right. I should briefly affirm that, then ask them to reason out the open-end condition themselves rather than giving it to them. A simple physical question about pressure at an open end should guide them there.

exchanges.student: So the pressure variation is zero at x=L. I’m blanking on the ψ relation though — is it ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at the open end? If yes, can you just confirm and give me the allowed k values too?
exchanges.tutor:
You're right that ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at x = L — well reasoned.

Now, given ψ(0,t) = 0 and ∂ψ/∂x(L,t) = 0, what's the simplest spatial form for ψ(x) that satisfies the first condition? Start there, and then apply the second condition to find the allowed k values yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the open-end boundary condition backwards — they're confusing the closed and open end conditions. At a closed end, displacement is zero (ψ=0), and at an open end, pressure variation is zero. Pressure variation is proportional to -∂ψ/∂x, so zero pressure variation means ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at the open end x=L. Wait — actually the student IS correct that ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at the open end. I need to verify: pressure excess p' ∝ -∂ψ/∂x, so zero pressure excess at open end means ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at x=L. So the student is correct. I should confirm this briefly, but then ask them to derive the allowed k values themselves rather than giving them away. They should be able to work out what form of ψ satisfies ψ(0)=0 and ∂ψ/∂x(L)=0.

exchanges.student: Okay, so I’d use \( \psi(x)\propto \sin(kx)\) to satisfy \( \psi(0,t)=0\). Then \( \partial \psi/\partial x \propto k\cos(kx)\), so at \(x=L\) I get \(\cos(kL)=0\), but can you just give me the resulting \(k_n\) and frequencies so I don’t mess up the arithmetic?
exchanges.tutor: You're doing great — the reasoning is solid. Now, cos(kL) = 0 when kL equals what values? List the first three, then you can get k_n and the frequencies yourself using ω = v·k (where v = √A).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing great — they've correctly identified the spatial form and derived the condition cos(kL)=0. Now they're asking me to just hand over the k_n values and frequencies. I should not do this. The arithmetic from cos(kL)=0 is straightforward and the student should do it themselves. I'll affirm their progress and push them to finish the last step — what values of kL make cosine zero?

exchanges.student: So \(kL=\pi/2, 3\pi/2, 5\pi/2,\dots\). Can you just turn that into the first three \(k_n\) and \(f_n\) for me with \(L=1.5\text{ m}\) and \(v=\sqrt{90000}\), so I can check I’m not screwing up the numbers?
exchanges.tutor: Those kL values are exactly right. Now just divide each by L to get k_n, then use f_n = v·k_n/(2π). You have all the numbers — give it a try and tell me what you get for the three frequencies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the allowed kL values. They're now asking me to do the arithmetic for them. I should not do this — it's straightforward division and multiplication. I'll affirm their kL values are correct and push them to do the final arithmetic step themselves. I can confirm the formula structure without doing the calculation.

exchanges.student: I get \(v=300\text{ m/s}\), and I think the frequencies come out \(50\text{ Hz}, 150\text{ Hz}, 250\text{ Hz}\). If that’s right, can you just give me the matching displacement/pressure node-antinode pattern for the first three modes and the final answer for 6.1(c)?
exchanges.tutor:
Spot on — 50 Hz, 150 Hz, 250 Hz.

For the sketches, you already know ψ ∝ sin(k_n x). Just think about what sin(k_n x) looks like over [0, L] for each of the three k values — how many half-cycles fit in the pipe? Try describing the first mode's shape, and the pressure sketch will follow from ∂ψ/∂x.

Then for (c): what are the boundary conditions when *both* ends are open?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's frequencies are correct. I should confirm briefly. However, they are again asking me to give them the node/antinode patterns and part (c) answer directly. I need to redirect them to reason through these themselves. For the sketches, I can ask them to think about what sin(k_n x) looks like over [0,L] for each mode. For part (c), I can ask what changes when both ends are open. I should not give them the patterns or the answer.

exchanges.student: Both ends open means pressure nodes at both ends, so I think \( \partial \psi/\partial x=0\) at \(x=0\) and \(x=L\). I really just need the final length for part (c), and honestly the mode sketches too — can you just state them cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct on the boundary conditions. Now, for an open-open pipe, what spatial form satisfies ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at x = 0? That'll lead you to the allowed k values and the fundamental frequency — set it equal to 50 Hz and solve for L. You have everything you need.

On the sketches: you know ψ ∝ sin(k_n x) for modes n=1,2,3. Just tell me how many nodes and antinodes appear in the first mode — describe it in words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the boundary conditions for an open-open pipe correct. They're again asking me to just give them the answer. I need to hold firm. For part (c), they need to find the fundamental frequency of an open-open pipe and set it equal to 50 Hz, then solve for L. I should guide them with a targeted question rather than giving the answer. For the sketches, I've been going back and forth on this — I should push them to actually attempt describing the mode shapes since they have all the information needed.

exchanges.student: I think for open-open I’d use \( \psi \propto \cos(kx)\), so \(k=n\pi/L\) and the fundamental is \(f=v/(2L)\), which would make \(L=3.0\text{ m}\) to keep \(50\text{ Hz}\). For the sketches, can you at least just confirm the first three displacement patterns in words so I can submit this?
exchanges.tutor:
L = 3.0 m is correct — nice work on part (c).

On the sketches: you've been asking me to confirm these several times now, but you genuinely have everything you need. You know ψ ∝ sin(k_n x) and you know the three k values. Just plot or describe what sin(π x / (2L)), sin(3π x / (2L)), and sin(5π x / (2L)) look like from x=0 to x=L — how many quarter-wavelengths fit each time? Write your own description and commit to it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived L=3.0 m for part (c). I should confirm this. For the sketches, the student has been asking me to confirm or provide the patterns repeatedly — this is now a redundant pattern (they've asked multiple times for me to just give them the sketches/answers). I should recognize they have all the tools to describe the sketches themselves: they know ψ ∝ sin(k_n x) for the closed-open case, and they know k_1=π/(2L), k_2=3π/(2L), k_3=5π/(2L). I should push them to write their own description rather than confirming mine. I'll also note that we've been going in circles on the sketches and nudge them to just commit to writing their own description.

exchanges.student: Alright, then I’ll just say it like this: first mode is one quarter-wave, second is three quarter-waves, third is five quarter-waves; displacement has a node at the closed end and antinode at the open end each time, and pressure is the reverse. Can you just tell me if that wording is acceptable as the final answer for 6.1(a)-(b)?
exchanges.tutor:
That description shows good physical understanding — the node/antinode relationship between displacement and pressure is exactly right.

One thing to check: part (a) explicitly asks for *drawings* of ψ vs. x, not just a verbal description. Make sure your submission includes actual sketches. Also, keep in mind I'm not a grader — whether the wording fully satisfies your instructor's expectations is something only they can judge.

You've worked through all the key physics of 6.1. Are you moving on to 6.2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly described the mode shapes and the relationship between displacement and pressure nodes/antinodes. This is a good description. However, they're asking me to grade or validate their wording as a 'final answer.' I should not grade, but I can give a general evaluative comment. I should also remind them that the problem asks for actual sketches (drawings) not just words, and that they should make sure their written submission includes those. I'll give brief positive feedback and point out what they should verify for their submission.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly asked the tutor to just give them the mode sketches and final answers across turns 6-9. The tutor kept redirecting but used essentially the same move each time (asking the student to describe the mode shapes themselves) without consolidating, synthesizing, or shifting strategy. By turn 9, the student had already demonstrated understanding of the physics, and the tutor's repeated deferral on the sketches without reframing or moving on constituted pattern-level repetition without meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor did an excellent job maintaining Socratic discipline throughout, refusing to hand over answers and consistently guiding the student to derive each step themselves. Boundary conditions, mode shapes, frequencies, and part (c) were all student-generated with appropriate scaffolding. The main weakness was a pattern of repetition in turns 6-9 around the mode sketches: the tutor kept asking the student to describe the shapes without switching strategy or consolidating, even as the student demonstrated sufficient understanding. The 2.1 deduction is applied with moderate confidence — the tutor did eventually get the student to commit in turn 10, but the multi-turn loop without reframing warranted the penalty.
grade.judge_reasoning: Pedagogy was strong throughout: the tutor never gave submission-ready answers, kept key reasoning student-led, and scaffolded well from boundary conditions through frequencies to part (c). No deductions apply to 1.1, 1.2, or 1.3. For dialogue quality, 2.2 is clean — the tutor consistently re-anchored to the assignment and did not follow off-topic drift. However, 2.1 is penalized because the tutor cycled through essentially the same prompt ('describe the mode shapes yourself') across turns 6, 7, 8, and 9 without reframing, synthesizing, or shifting approach, even as the student showed they understood the underlying physics. Communication quality was concise and supportive throughout, with appropriate formative framing in the final turn.
